ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for lobster floating noodle (ee-fu noodle with lobster). FOODON_03311737 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for lobster noodle as a composite dish. A0CKP (Crustaceans, cooked) is used as the closest parent anchor for the primary protein component. The noodle component is not separately coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for lobster floating noodle as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of lobster floating noodle is approximately 350 g, comprising half a small lobster with braised ee-fu noodles in a savoury sauce, served as a main dish for one person.

nutrient_source.title: Lobster, cooked (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #15149) and Noodles, egg, cooked (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #20113); composite estimation weighted 40% lobster cooked, 50% ee-fu noodle cooked, 10% sauce/oil
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for lobster floating noodle exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are estimated from a weighted composite: 40 g cooked lobster (USDA SR Legacy #15149: energy 98 kcal, protein 20.5 g, fat 0.6 g, carb 1.3 g per 100 g), 50 g cooked ee-fu/egg noodle (USDA SR Legacy #20113: energy 138 kcal, protein 4.5 g, fat 2.1 g, carb 25.2 g per 100 g), and 10 g sauce/oil contribution (estimated 45 kcal, protein 0.5 g, fat 3.5 g, carb 2.0 g per 10 g). Final per-100 g values derived from this 100 g composite model.

gi_source.title: Noodles, instant, cooked (GI approximately 40–47); egg noodles cooked GI ~40; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lobster floating noodle (ee-fu noodle with lobster). GI estimated at 40 based on cooked egg noodles / ee-fu noodles, which are the dominant glycemic carbohydrate component. The University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) report GI of cooked egg noodles at approximately 40 (low GI). The lobster and sauce components contribute negligible glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 14.2 × 3.5) / 100 = 19.9 g (serving size 350 g, carbohydrate per serving = 49.7 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from oyster sauce, soy sauce, and stock
hypertension.guidance: A 350 g restaurant serving provides approximately 1470 mg sodium, which represents 64–98% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 1500–2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sauce preparation or limit portion size to manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from ee-fu noodles with low GI
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI of approximately 40 from the ee-fu noodle base supports a moderate postprandial glucose response; however, the glycemic load per full restaurant serving (~19.9 g) is moderate, and individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a half-portion or substitute with lower-carbohydrate noodle options.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol from lobster and sodium from sauces
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lobster provides lean protein with low saturated fat (approximately 0.4 g per 100 g), which is favourable for cardiovascular health, but the high sodium content from restaurant-style sauces poses a risk for blood pressure management in individuals with cardiovascular disease; portion control and sauce reduction are advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lobster Floating Noodle (龙虾浮面, Mi Terapung Udang Karang) is a premium Singaporean-Malaysian Zi Char seafood noodle dish featuring whole or halved fresh local lobster served atop braised ee-fu noodles (yi mian), which are soft, pre-fried egg noodles that absorb the rich savoury sauce. The dish is prepared by first steaming or briefly cooking the lobster, then braising the ee-fu noodles in a flavourful stock-based sauce made with oyster sauce, soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, and ginger. The noodles are described as 'floating' because they are presented in a generous pool of glossy, umami-rich sauce. The texture contrasts tender, sweet lobster meat with soft, silky noodles. This dish is a signature offering at Zi Char restaurants and seafood houses in Singapore, particularly at establishments along Boat Quay and East Coast Seafood Centre. Nutritionally, it provides a moderate energy density (~118 kcal per 100 g), high-quality protein from lobster (~10.7 g per 100 g), and moderate carbohydrates from the noodle base (~14.2 g per 100 g). The low GI (~40) of the ee-fu noodle component supports a moderate postprandial glucose response. Sodium content is elevated due to restaurant-style saucing, making it a moderate concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The dish is a good source of vitamin B12, selenium, and zinc from the lobster component.
common_user_queries: How many calories in lobster floating noodle?
Is lobster noodle high in sodium?
What is Zi Char lobster noodle?
Is lobster floating noodle suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of ee-fu noodles?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for lobster floating noodle was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy data for cooked lobster and cooked egg noodles, with a sauce/oil contribution estimate. GI is estimated from analogous cooked egg noodle data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach rather than a direct food match.
notes: Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked lobster (#15149) and cooked egg noodles (#20113), weighted at 40:50:10 (lobster:noodle:sauce). Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this composite dish in the source databases. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data for this composite. Trans fat is confirmed at 0.0 g as lobster and egg noodles are not sources of industrial trans fats (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central). The dish is not certified halal as lobster preparation and sauce ingredients at individual restaurants may vary. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on cooked egg noodles (ee-fu noodle base) per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). GL per serving = (40 × 49.7) / 100 = 19.9 g for a 350 g serving. The food_name has been normalised to 'Lobster Floating Noodle' as the canonical English name, removing the restaurant-specific 'Marina Signature' prefix per Veritas naming rules.
